question:
\[
S(\alpha) = \{ \lfloor n\alpha \rfloor : n = 1,2,3,\dots \}.
\]
Prove that $\{1,2,3,\dots\}$ cannot be expressed as the disjoint
union of three sets $S(\alpha), S(\beta)$ and $S(\gamma)$. [As
usual, $\lfloor x \rfloor$ is the greatest integer $\leq x$.]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
Obviously $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ have to be greater than 1, and no
two can both be rational, so without loss of generality assume that
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are irrational.
Let $\{x\} = x - \lfloor x \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $x$.
Then $m \in S(\alpha)$ if and only if $f(m/\alpha) \in (1-1/\alpha,1)
\cup \{0\}$. In particular, this means that $S(\alpha) \cap \{1,
\dots, n\}$ contains $\lceil (n+1)/\alpha \rceil -1$ elements, and
similarly. Hence for every integer $n$,
\[
n = \left\lceil \frac{n+1}\alpha \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{n+1}\beta \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{n+1}\gamma \right\rceil -3.
\]
Dividing through by $n$ and taking the limit as $n \to \infty$ shows
that $1/\alpha + 1/\beta + 1/\gamma = 1$. That in turn implies that
for all $n$,
\[
\left\{ - \frac{n+1}{\alpha} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{n+1}{\beta} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{n+1}{\gamma} \right\} = 2.
\]
Our desired contradiction is equivalent to showing that the left side actually
takes the value 1 for some $n$. Since the left side is
an integer, it suffices to show that $\{ -(n+1)/\alpha\} +
\{-(n+1)/\beta\} < 1$ for some $n$.

A result in ergodic theory (the two-dimensional version of the Weil
equidistribution theorem) states that if $1,r,s$ are linearly
independent over the rationals, then the set of points $(\{nr\},
\{ns\}$ is dense (and in fact equidistributed) in the unit square. In
particular, our claim definitely holds unless $a/\alpha + b/\beta =
c$ for some integers $a,b,c$.

On the other hand, suppose that such a relation does hold. Since
$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are irrational, by the one-dimensional Weil
theorem, the set of points $(\{-n/\alpha\}, \{-n/\beta\}$ is dense in
the set of $(x,y)$ in the unit square such that $ax + by$ is an integer.
It is simple enough to show that this set meets the region $\{(x,y)
\in [0,1]^{2}: x+y<1\}$ unless $a+b$ is an integer, and that would
imply that $1/\alpha + 1/\beta$, a quantity between 0 and 1, is an
integer. We have our desired contradiction.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
\[
setfunc(alphavar) = \{ \lfloor indexer\,alphavar \rfloor : indexer = 1,2,3,\dots \}.
\]
Prove that $\{1,2,3,\dots\}$ cannot be expressed as the disjoint
union of three sets $setfunc(alphavar), setfunc(betavar)$ and $setfunc(gammavar)$. [As
usual, $\lfloor placeholder \rfloor$ is the greatest integer $\leq placeholder$.]
descriptive_long.solution:
Obviously $alphavar, betavar, gammavar$ have to be greater than 1, and no
two can both be rational, so without loss of generality assume that
$alphavar$ and $betavar$ are irrational.
Let $\{placeholder\} = placeholder - \lfloor placeholder \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $placeholder$.
Then $member \in setfunc(alphavar)$ if and only if $f(member/alphavar) \in (1-1/alphavar,1)
\cup \{0\}$. In particular, this means that $setfunc(alphavar) \cap \{1,
\dots, indexer\}$ contains $\lceil (indexer+1)/alphavar \rceil -1$ elements, and
similarly. Hence for every integer $indexer$,
\[
indexer = \left\lceil \frac{indexer+1}{alphavar} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{indexer+1}{betavar} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{indexer+1}{gammavar} \right\rceil -3.
\]
Dividing through by $indexer$ and taking the limit as $indexer \to \infty$ shows
that $1/alphavar + 1/betavar + 1/gammavar = 1$. That in turn implies that
for all $indexer$,
\[
\left\{ - \frac{indexer+1}{alphavar} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{indexer+1}{betavar} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{indexer+1}{gammavar} \right\} = 2.
\]
Our desired contradiction is equivalent to showing that the left side actually
takes the value 1 for some $indexer$. Since the left side is
an integer, it suffices to show that $\{ -(indexer+1)/alphavar\} +
\{-(indexer+1)/betavar\} < 1$ for some $indexer$.

A result in ergodic theory (the two-dimensional version of the Weil
equidistribution theorem) states that if $1,density,sequence$ are linearly
independent over the rationals, then the set of points $(\{indexer\,density\},
\{indexer\,sequence\})$ is dense (and in fact equidistributed) in the unit square. In
particular, our claim definitely holds unless $coeffone/alphavar + coefftwo/betavar =
coeffthr$ for some integers $coeffone,coefftwo,coeffthr$.

On the other hand, suppose that such a relation does hold. Since
$alphavar$ and $betavar$ are irrational, by the one-dimensional Weil
theorem, the set of points $(\{-indexer/alphavar\}, \{-indexer/betavar\})$ is dense in
the set of $(placeholder,y)$ in the unit square such that $coeffone\,placeholder + coefftwo\,y$ is an integer.
It is simple enough to show that this set meets the region $\{(placeholder,y)
\in [0,1]^{2}: placeholder+y<1\}$ unless $coeffone+coefftwo$ is an integer, and that would
imply that $1/alphavar + 1/betavar$, a quantity between 0 and 1, is an
integer. We have our desired contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
\[
windswept(evergreen) = \{ \lfloor hummingbird evergreen \rfloor : hummingbird = 1,2,3,\dots \}.
\]
Prove that $\{1,2,3,\dots\}$ cannot be expressed as the disjoint
union of three sets $windswept(evergreen), windswept(lighthouse)$ and $windswept(raincloud)$. [As
usual, $\lfloor sandstorm \rfloor$ is the greatest integer $\leq sandstorm$.]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Obviously evergreen, lighthouse, raincloud have to be greater than 1, and no
two can both be rational, so without loss of generality assume that
evergreen and lighthouse are irrational.
Let $\{sandstorm\} = sandstorm - \lfloor sandstorm \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of sandstorm.
Then $pinecones \in windswept(evergreen)$ if and only if $f(pinecones/evergreen) \in (1-1/evergreen,1)
\cup \{0\}$. In particular, this means that $windswept(evergreen) \cap \{1,
\dots, hummingbird\}$ contains $\lceil (hummingbird+1)/evergreen \rceil -1$ elements, and
similarly. Hence for every integer $hummingbird$,
\[
hummingbird = \left\lceil \frac{hummingbird+1}{evergreen} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{hummingbird+1}{lighthouse} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{hummingbird+1}{raincloud} \right\rceil -3.
\]
Dividing through by $hummingbird$ and taking the limit as $hummingbird \to \infty$ shows
that $1/evergreen + 1/lighthouse + 1/raincloud = 1$. That in turn implies that
for all $hummingbird$,
\[
\left\{ - \frac{hummingbird+1}{evergreen} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{hummingbird+1}{lighthouse} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{hummingbird+1}{raincloud} \right\} = 2.
\]
Our desired contradiction is equivalent to showing that the left side actually
takes the value 1 for some $hummingbird$. Since the left side is
an integer, it suffices to show that $\{ -(hummingbird+1)/evergreen\} +
\{-(hummingbird+1)/lighthouse\} < 1$ for some $hummingbird$.

A result in ergodic theory (the two-dimensional version of the Weil
equidistribution theorem) states that if $1,watershed,driftwood$ are linearly
independent over the rationals, then the set of points $(\{hummingbird watershed\},
\{hummingbird driftwood\}$ is dense (and in fact equidistributed) in the unit square. In
particular, our claim definitely holds unless $moonlight/evergreen + starlight/lighthouse =
afterglow$ for some integers $moonlight,starlight,afterglow$.

On the other hand, suppose that such a relation does hold. Since
evergreen and lighthouse are irrational, by the one-dimensional Weil
theorem, the set of points $(\{-hummingbird/evergreen\}, \{-hummingbird/lighthouse\}$ is dense in
the set of $(sandstorm,y)$ in the unit square such that $moonlight sandstorm + starlight y$ is an integer.
It is simple enough to show that this set meets the region $\{(sandstorm,y)
\in [0,1]^{2}: sandstorm+y<1\}$ unless $moonlight+starlight$ is an integer, and that would
imply that $1/evergreen + 1/lighthouse$, a quantity between 0 and 1, is an
integer. We have our desired contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
\[
sequence(smallvalue) = \{ \lfloor fractional smallvalue \rfloor : fractional = 1,2,3,\dots \}.
\]
Prove that $\{1,2,3,\dots\}$ cannot be expressed as the disjoint
union of three sets $sequence(smallvalue), sequence(tinyvalue)$ and $sequence(minuscule)$. [As
usual, $\lfloor constant \rfloor$ is the greatest integer $\leq constant$.]

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\<<<
Obviously $smallvalue, tinyvalue, minuscule$ have to be greater than 1, and no
two can both be rational, so without loss of generality assume that
$smallvalue$ and $tinyvalue$ are irrational.
Let $\{constant\} = constant - \lfloor constant \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $constant$.
Then $irrational \in sequence(smallvalue)$ if and only if $f(irrational/smallvalue) \in (1-1/smallvalue,1)
\cup \{0\}$. In particular, this means that $sequence(smallvalue) \cap \{1,
\dots, fractional\}$ contains $\lceil (fractional+1)/smallvalue \rceil -1$ elements, and
similarly. Hence for every integer $fractional$,
\[
fractional = \left\lceil \frac{fractional+1}{smallvalue} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{fractional+1}{tinyvalue} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{fractional+1}{minuscule} \right\rceil -3.
\]
Dividing through by $fractional$ and taking the limit as $fractional \to \infty$ shows
that $1/smallvalue + 1/tinyvalue + 1/minuscule = 1$. That in turn implies that
for all $fractional$,
\[
\left\{ - \frac{fractional+1}{smallvalue} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{fractional+1}{tinyvalue} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{fractional+1}{minuscule} \right\} = 2.
\]
Our desired contradiction is equivalent to showing that the left side actually
takes the value 1 for some $fractional$. Since the left side is
an integer, it suffices to show that $\{ -(fractional+1)/smallvalue\} +
\{-(fractional+1)/tinyvalue\} < 1$ for some $fractional$.

A result in ergodic theory (the two-dimensional version of the Weil
equidistribution theorem) states that if $1,imaginary,disorderly$ are linearly
independent over the rationals, then the set of points $(\{fractionalimaginary\},
\{fractionaldisorderly\}$ is dense (and in fact equidistributed) in the unit square. In
particular, our claim definitely holds unless $variable/smallvalue + stableone/tinyvalue =
changing$ for some integers $variable,stableone,changing$.

On the other hand, suppose that such a relation does hold. Since
$smallvalue$ and $tinyvalue$ are irrational, by the one-dimensional Weil
theorem, the set of points $(\{-fractional/smallvalue\}, \{-fractional/tinyvalue\}$ is dense in
the set of $(constant,y)$ in the unit square such that $variable constant + stableone y$ is an integer.
It is simple enough to show that this set meets the region $\{(constant,y)
\in [0,1]^{2}: constant+y<1\}$ unless $variable+stableone$ is an integer, and that would
imply that $1/smallvalue + 1/tinyvalue$, a quantity between 0 and 1, is an
integer. We have our desired contradiction.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
>>>

garbled_string.question:
\[
pnsjdwkt(azldkqwe) = \{ \lfloor qzxwvtnp azldkqwe \rfloor : qzxwvtnp = 1,2,3,\dots \}.
\]
Prove that $\{1,2,3,\dots\}$ cannot be expressed as the disjoint
union of three sets $pnsjdwkt(azldkqwe), pnsjdwkt(kmdzpryw)$ and $pnsjdwkt(cxvtrhgu)$. [As
usual, $\lfloor vkjzmqer \rfloor$ is the greatest integer $\leq vkjzmqer$.]
garbled_string.solution:
Obviously $azldkqwe, kmdzpryw, cxvtrhgu$ have to be greater than 1, and no
two can both be rational, so without loss of generality assume that
$azldkqwe$ and $kmdzpryw$ are irrational.
Let $\{vkjzmqer\} = vkjzmqer - \lfloor vkjzmqer \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $vkjzmqer$.
Then $hjgrksla \in pnsjdwkt(azldkqwe)$ if and only if $f(hjgrksla/azldkqwe) \in (1-1/azldkqwe,1)
\cup \{0\}$. In particular, this means that $pnsjdwkt(azldkqwe) \cap \{1,
\dots, qzxwvtnp\}$ contains $\lceil (qzxwvtnp+1)/azldkqwe \rceil -1$ elements, and
similarly. Hence for every integer $qzxwvtnp$,
\[
qzxwvtnp = \left\lceil \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{azldkqwe} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{kmdzpryw} \right\rceil +
 \left\lceil \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{cxvtrhgu} \right\rceil -3.
\]
Dividing through by $qzxwvtnp$ and taking the limit as $qzxwvtnp \to \infty$ shows
that $1/azldkqwe + 1/kmdzpryw + 1/cxvtrhgu = 1$. That in turn implies that
for all $qzxwvtnp$,
\[
\left\{ - \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{azldkqwe} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{kmdzpryw} \right\} +
\left\{ - \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{cxvtrhgu} \right\} = 2.
\]
Our desired contradiction is equivalent to showing that the left side actually
takes the value 1 for some $qzxwvtnp$. Since the left side is
an integer, it suffices to show that $\{ -(qzxwvtnp+1)/azldkqwe\} +
\{-(qzxwvtnp+1)/kmdzpryw\} < 1$ for some $qzxwvtnp$.

A result in ergodic theory (the two-dimensional version of the Weil
equidistribution theorem) states that if $1,opdynmcl,wbrtqfsa$ are linearly
independent over the rationals, then the set of points $(\{qzxwvtnp opdynmcl\},
\{qzxwvtnp wbrtqfsa\}$ is dense (and in fact equidistributed) in the unit square. In
particular, our claim definitely holds unless $tlmcxoph/azldkqwe + zqwrvekl/kmdzpryw =
yplhndgt$ for some integers $tlmcxoph,zqwrvekl,yplhndgt$.

On the other hand, suppose that such a relation does hold. Since
$azldkqwe$ and $kmdzpryw$ are irrational, by the one-dimensional Weil
theorem, the set of points $(\{-qzxwvtnp/azldkqwe\}, \{-qzxwvtnp/kmdzpryw\}$ is dense in
the set of $(vkjzmqer,y)$ in the unit square such that $tlmcxoph vkjzmqer + zqwrvekl y$ is an integer.
It is simple enough to show that this set meets the region $\{(vkjzmqer,y)
\in [0,1]^{2}: vkjzmqer+y<1\}$ unless $tlmcxoph+zqwrvekl$ is an integer, and that would
imply that $1/azldkqwe + 1/kmdzpryw$, a quantity between 0 and 1, is an
integer. We have our desired contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
For each index \kappa  \in  {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta } choose a real pair  

  \theta \kappa  > 1 and 0 < \varphi \kappa  < 1.

Define  
  T\kappa  := {\lfloor n \theta \kappa  + \varphi \kappa \rfloor  : n = 1,2,3,\ldots }.                ()

Can the positive integers be written as a disjoint union of the four shifted Beatty sets,

  \mathbb{N} = T\alpha  \sqcup  T\beta  \sqcup  T\gamma  \sqcup  T\delta  ?

Prove that such a decomposition is impossible, even if the four shifts \varphi \kappa  are chosen arbitrarily (they need not sum to an integer, coincide, etc.).
kernel_variant.solution:
(\approx  320 words)  
Assume, toward a contradiction, that

 (0) \mathbb{N} = T\alpha  \sqcup  T\beta  \sqcup  T\gamma  \sqcup  T\delta ,  \theta \kappa  > 1, 0<\varphi \kappa <1.

Step 1 - How many terms of T\kappa  do not exceed N.  
Put  

 A\kappa (N) := |T\kappa  \cap  {1,\ldots ,N}|,  N \geq  1.

Membership m \in  T\kappa  means m = \lfloor n \theta \kappa +\varphi \kappa \rfloor  for some n, hence  

 n \theta \kappa +\varphi \kappa  < m+1 \leq  N+1 \Leftrightarrow  n < (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa .

Thus  

 A\kappa (N)=\lceil (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa \rceil -1
    =\lfloor (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa \rfloor -\varepsilon \kappa (N),

where \varepsilon \kappa (N)=1 precisely when (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa  is an integer, and 0 otherwise.  
Because of (0),

 \sum \kappa  A\kappa (N)=N.                                                        (1)

Inserting the formula for A\kappa (N) gives  

 \sum \kappa  \lfloor (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa \rfloor =N+Z(N),                                        (2)

with Z(N):=\sum \kappa  \varepsilon \kappa (N) \in  {0,1,2,3,4}.

Step 2 - The reciprocal identity.  
Divide (2) by N and let N\to \infty : lim \lfloor x\rfloor /N=x/N, hence  

 (3) 1/\alpha +1/\beta +1/\gamma +1/\delta  = 1.

Step 3 - A rigid fractional-part sum.  
Write { x }=x-\lfloor x\rfloor .  From (2) and the trivial equality  

 \sum \kappa  (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa  = N+1-\sum \kappa  \varphi \kappa ,

subtracting yields  

 \sum \kappa  {(N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa } = 1-\sum \kappa  \varphi \kappa  - Z(N).                        (4)

The left side always lies in [0,4), so the right side must as well; consequently

 Z(N)=0 for every N.                                            (5)

Therefore (N+1-\varphi \kappa )/\theta \kappa  is never an integer; in particular, any \theta \kappa  with an even reduced denominator is excluded.

Step 4 - An unavoidable visit to the triangle x+y<1.  
Define

 xN:={-(N+1-\varphi \alpha )/\alpha }, yN:={-(N+1-\varphi \beta )/\beta }.

Because xN+1\equiv xN-1/\alpha  (mod 1) and the analogous relation for yN, the sequence (xN,yN) is the orbit of v0:= (-(1-\varphi \alpha )/\alpha , -(1-\varphi \beta )/\beta ) under the toral translation \tau (x,y)=(x-1/\alpha , y-1/\beta ).

Let \Delta := {(x,y)\in [0,1)^2 : x+y<1}.  If some index N satisfies (xN,yN)\in \Delta , then

 {-(N+1-\varphi \alpha )/\alpha }+{-(N+1-\varphi \beta )/\beta }<1,                              (6)

and combining (4),(5) yields

 sum of all four fractional parts = 1-\sum \kappa  \varphi \kappa  < 3,

contradicting (4).  Hence no orbit point may enter \Delta .

Yet \tau  is an ergodic translation unless 1,1/\alpha ,1/\beta  are rationally dependent.  In the ergodic case the orbit is dense and certainly meets \Delta , contradiction.

If a linear relation a/\alpha +b/\beta =c (integers, coprime) exists, then, by (5), neither 1/\alpha  nor 1/\beta  is integral; at least one is irrational, so \tau  restricted to the closed subgroup L={ax+by\equiv \lambda  (mod 1)} (\lambda  determined by v0) is again ergodic, forcing an intersection with \Delta .  

Finally, when both 1/\alpha =q/p and 1/\beta =s/r are rational with odd denominators p,r\geq 3, the orbit is strictly periodic of length lcm(p,r).  Averaging xN+yN over one period gives 1; therefore some term is <1, placing the orbit in \Delta  - the same contradiction.

Every possible arithmetic configuration leads to a violation of (4), so assumption (0) is impossible. \blacksquare

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
